text: Hi, this is Dr. Elizabeth Coronado, and I want to talk to you today about the technique and safety steps and benefits of removing fibroid tumors robotically. This surgery is called a robotic myomectomy. It's important to choose a surgeon with good experience when deciding on a myomectomy. This woman has at least seven fibroids that we're going to remove.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to inject diluted vasopressin into the tissue, causing it to change color from pink to white as the blood vessels constrict, reducing potential bleeding.

Prior to the procedure, one of the most important safety steps that I've done is to order an MRI so that we know exactly where this woman's fibroids are located in her uterus. I've also given her a medication prior to the procedure to help reduce the risk of bleeding and improve the fibroids tightness as they are delivered out of the muscle. And then right now she's receiving an infusion of another medicine called TXA that helps reduce her bleeding during surgery.
The surgeon uses scissors to remove the largest fibroid, while a robotic instrument, referred to as the 'third arm,' is visible at the top of the screen in a locked position.

So I'm pursuing these fibroids that I've identified on the MRI because we want to help this woman with her symptoms. This woman has had heavy bleeding and a sensation of a really large mass in her pelvis. Her pants have gotten tight over time and her periods are becoming overwhelming. This procedure is going to be awesome for her.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to sew a small fibroid to a larger one, creating a 'pearls on a string' configuration to ensure all fibroids are removed together in a contained bag. The needle is tucked away safely for future use.

The surgeon reviews safety steps, including pre-surgery medications, vasopressin injection to constrict blood vessels, and MRI guidance. The largest fibroid is visible, deeply embedded in the uterine tissue, with the MRI aiding in identifying the entry point for access.
The surgeon carefully removes the fibroid from the uterine muscle, avoiding penetration into the uterine lining to preserve fertility potential.

The surgeon holds up the fallopian tube, which is positioned close to a fibroid tumor on the right side. Careful manipulation is performed to protect the fallopian tube from potential scar tissue formation during the procedure.
The surgeon sews the defect closed in multiple layers at the site of the incision where fibroids were removed, ensuring hemostasis and preventing bleeding within the uterine muscle layers.

The surgeon uses scissors in the right hand and a grasper in the left to manipulate the fibroid at the front of the uterus, while the third arm holds the tumor at the top. The assistant uses a tenaculum to assist in removing the fibroid, carefully preserving uterine tissue and avoiding the nearby fallopian tube.
I also offer other techniques for treatment of fibroids, including the ASESA ablation of fibroids through radio frequency technology. Keep an eye out on this YouTube for videos of that coming soon.

The surgeon identifies a fibroid on the backside of the uterus near the ovary, with the ovary visible as a white structure hanging down alongside the fallopian tube. The surgeon notes minimal blood loss during the procedure and proceeds to sew up the sites where fibroids were removed, using delicate stitches near the fallopian tube to preserve its integrity and optimize fertility chances.

The surgeon sprays a liquid over the incisions made during the myomectomy to prevent bleeding and promote blood clotting at these sites, addressing the significant risk of bleeding associated with the procedure.

The surgeon uses a strong containment bag placed in the abdomen to safely remove fibroids. The fibroids are placed inside the bag, which is then extracted through a small incision at the belly button. The fibroids are cut into smaller pieces within the bag to ensure a minimally invasive and cosmetic result.
